rules.title: CCPA / CPRA screening
rules.focus: California consumer privacy rights and transparency obligations

remediations.action: Document consumer rights request handling and notice transparency.
Map personal and sensitive data categories relevant to California residents.
Maintain evidence of consumer request handling and disclosures.

applicability.reasonLikely: Processing personal data of US consumers may trigger state privacy obligations such as CCPA / CPRA.
applicability.reasonMaybe: US-linked processing infrastructure may require further privacy screening.

questions.title: Do you provide mechanisms for US consumers to request access or deletion of personal data?
questions.help: Consumer rights handling is a central expectation in modern US privacy laws.

questions.title: Do you maintain a 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' mechanism for California residents?
questions.help: CCPA / CPRA requires a clear opt-out for the sale or sharing of personal information.

questions.title: Does your privacy notice disclose all categories of personal information collected and the purposes for which it is used?
questions.help: CCPA requires businesses to inform consumers at or before the point of collection.

questions.title: Do you honour opt-out requests within 15 business days?
questions.help: CPRA sets a 15 business day timeline to comply with opt-out requests.

questions.title: Do you have a process to respond to verified consumer rights requests within 45 days?
questions.help: CCPA / CPRA requires businesses to respond to access, deletion, and correction requests within 45 days.

rules.title: US state privacy laws screening
rules.focus: Emerging state privacy laws beyond California

remediations.action: Screen US operations for additional state privacy obligations.

applicability.reasonMaybe: Several US states have introduced privacy laws that may apply depending on user base and revenue thresholds.

questions.title: Do you track which US states your customers or users are located in?
questions.help: State-level privacy laws depend on the residency of consumers, not the location of your business.

questions.title: Have you assessed whether your business meets the thresholds that trigger state privacy laws (e.g. 100,000+ consumers or revenue from data sales)?
questions.help: Most US state privacy laws apply only above certain revenue or volume thresholds.

questions.title: Have you mapped which states represent your largest user concentrations to prioritise compliance scope?
questions.help: Prioritising by user volume reduces the cost of multi-state compliance efforts.

applicability.reasonLikely: Processing children's data in online services may trigger COPPA obligations.

questions.title: Do you obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting personal data from children under 13?
questions.help: COPPA requires verifiable parental consent prior to collecting, using, or disclosing personal information from children.

questions.title: Do you maintain a children's privacy notice that discloses what information is collected from children and how it is used?
questions.help: A clear and prominent children's privacy notice is a core COPPA requirement.

questions.title: Do you provide parents with the ability to review and delete their child's personal data?
questions.help: COPPA grants parents the right to review and request deletion of their child's information.

rules.title: FTC unfair or deceptive practices screening
rules.focus: Consumer protection enforcement related to data practices

applicability.reasonLikely: FTC enforcement can apply to deceptive privacy or security practices affecting US consumers.

questions.title: Do your privacy representations accurately reflect actual data practices?
questions.help: Misleading privacy claims — including in privacy notices, marketing, or consent flows — may trigger FTC enforcement.

questions.title: Do you maintain reasonable security measures proportionate to the sensitivity of data you hold?
questions.help: The FTC treats failure to maintain reasonable security as an unfair practice.

questions.title: Have you reviewed third-party integrations and data-sharing arrangements to ensure they reflect disclosed practices?
questions.help: Undisclosed data sharing with third parties has been a recurring basis for FTC enforcement actions.

officialSources.label: National Conference of State Legislatures — Breach Laws

applicability.reasonLikely: US states require notification to individuals and regulators following certain data breaches.

questions.title: Do you maintain a documented process for notifying affected individuals after a data breach?
questions.help: All 50 US states have breach notification laws. Timelines range from 30 to 90 days depending on state.

questions.title: Do you know which states require regulator or attorney general notification in addition to individual notice?
questions.help: Many states require notification to state regulators alongside consumer notification.

questions.title: Is your breach response plan tested at least annually?
questions.help: Regulators increasingly expect documented and tested incident response capabilities.

rules.title: Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act
rules.focus: Consumer privacy rights and obligations for businesses operating in Virginia

remediations.action: Assess whether Virginia resident volumes meet VCDPA thresholds and implement required consumer rights workflows.
Maintain data protection assessments for high-risk processing activities.

applicability.reasonLikely: Businesses processing personal data of Virginia residents may be subject to VCDPA.
applicability.reasonMaybe: Services offered to US consumers may involve Virginia residents.

questions.title: Have you assessed whether your business meets VCDPA thresholds (100,000+ Virginia consumers or 25,000+ consumers with 50%+ revenue from data sales)?
questions.help: VCDPA applies to controllers that meet these thresholds, regardless of where the business is incorporated.

questions.title: Do you provide Virginia consumers with rights to access, correct, delete, and obtain a portable copy of their personal data?
questions.help: VCDPA grants consumers five core rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, and opt-out.

questions.title: Have you completed data protection assessments for high-risk processing activities (targeted advertising, sale of data, profiling)?
questions.help: VCDPA requires written data protection assessments before conducting these processing activities.

rules.title: Colorado Privacy Act
rules.focus: Consumer privacy rights and data processing obligations

remediations.action: Implement universal opt-out mechanism for Colorado residents and document consent workflows.

applicability.reasonLikely: Businesses processing personal data of Colorado residents may be subject to the Colorado Privacy Act.

questions.title: Do you honour a universal opt-out signal (such as the Global Privacy Control) from Colorado residents?
questions.help: The Colorado Privacy Act uniquely requires businesses to honour universal opt-out mechanisms like GPC.

questions.title: Do you provide Colorado consumers with the right to opt out of targeted advertising, sale of personal data, and profiling for significant decisions?
questions.help: CPA grants consumers explicit opt-out rights for these three processing activities.

questions.title: Have you completed data protection assessments before engaging in high-risk processing activities involving Colorado residents?
questions.help: CPA requires documented data protection assessments for targeted advertising, data sales, and profiling.

remediations.action: Review Connecticut resident volumes and implement consumer rights request workflows.

applicability.reasonLikely: Businesses processing personal data of Connecticut residents may be subject to CTDPA.

questions.title: Do you provide Connecticut consumers with rights to access, correct, delete, and obtain portable copies of their personal data?
questions.help: The CTDPA grants consumers the same five core rights as Virginia's VCDPA.

questions.title: Do you honour opt-out requests for targeted advertising, sale of personal data, and profiling from Connecticut residents?
questions.help: CTDPA requires businesses to provide consumers with the ability to opt out of these processing activities.

questions.title: Does your privacy notice disclose your data processing practices clearly enough for Connecticut consumers to exercise their rights?
questions.help: A clear, accessible privacy notice is a prerequisite for meeting CTDPA transparency obligations.

remediations.action: Review applicability thresholds and implement consumer rights workflows for Utah residents.

applicability.reasonLikely: Businesses processing personal data of Utah residents above revenue thresholds may be subject to UCPA.

questions.title: Does your business meet UCPA thresholds ($25M+ annual revenue and 100,000+ Utah consumers, or 25,000+ consumers with 50%+ revenue from data sales)?
questions.help: UCPA applies only to businesses above these thresholds, making it narrower than some other state laws.

questions.title: Do you provide Utah consumers with rights to access, delete, and obtain a portable copy of their personal data?
questions.help: UCPA grants consumers access, deletion, and portability rights, but does not include a correction right.

questions.title: Do you provide Utah consumers with the ability to opt out of the sale of personal data and targeted advertising?
questions.help: UCPA requires opt-out rights for data sale and targeted advertising.

rules.title: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act
rules.focus: Consumer privacy rights and processor obligations

officialSources.label: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act

remediations.action: Assess TDPSA applicability based on Texas resident volumes and implement consumer rights workflows.
Maintain data processing agreements with processors that reflect TDPSA requirements.

applicability.reasonLikely: Businesses processing personal data of Texas residents may be subject to the TDPSA.

questions.title: Do you provide Texas consumers with rights to access, correct, delete, and obtain portable copies of their personal data?
questions.help: The TDPSA grants consumers five core rights comparable to other state laws, including a correction right.

questions.title: Do you provide Texas consumers with the ability to opt out of targeted advertising, sale of personal data, and profiling for significant decisions?
questions.help: TDPSA requires businesses to honour opt-out requests for these processing activities.

questions.title: Do your contracts with data processors include the protections required under TDPSA?
questions.help: TDPSA requires written contracts with processors that include specific obligations around data handling and security.
